eras.label: Ranchland
eras.blurb: Verdugo Canyon before the city: a wagon road, a rail line, scattered ranch houses. From the USGS Pasadena topographic map, surveyed 1894–1900 — no aerial photographs exist this early.
eras.credit: USGS Historical Topographic Map Collection, Pasadena quadrangle (1900 ed.), public domain

eras.label: Bare hillside
eras.blurb: Glendale Junior College is one year old, meeting in borrowed high-school rooms downtown. The future campus site is empty foothill land; speculative street grids are freshly graded in the flats.
eras.credit: Fairchild Aerial Surveys flight C-300, Jan 1928. Courtesy of UCSB Library Geospatial Collection.

eras.label: The new campus
eras.blurb: The brand-new Verdugo Road campus, opened 1937 — built with PWA funds and WPA-graded grounds after three years of classes held in tent bungalows.
eras.credit: USDA flight AXJ-1938, June 1938. Courtesy of UCSB Library Geospatial Collection.

eras.label: Postwar boom
eras.blurb: Subdivisions fill the valley and climb the lower slopes. Verdugo Road — then signed as State Route 2 — is the busy surface highway past campus. The canyon behind campus is still wild.
eras.credit: Flight C-22555, Aug 22 1956. Courtesy of UCSB Library Geospatial Collection.

eras.label: The freeway cut
eras.blurb: The Glendale Freeway is carved through the hills. Fresh grading gleams at the 134 interchange. This view is a composite: 1971 photography where it exists, filled with 1968 imagery in a band near campus that the 1971 flight never captured.
eras.credit: Teledyne Geotronics flights TG-2755 (Apr 3 1971) and TG-2400 (Mar 19 1968). Courtesy of UCSB Library Geospatial Collection.

eras.label: Freeway open
eras.blurb: The 2 finally opened in spring 1978 — its last segment sat finished but closed for years, famous as a film location. The modern street pattern is complete.
eras.credit: Teledyne Geotronics flight TG-3800, Apr 28 1981. Courtesy of UCSB Library Geospatial Collection.

eras.label: The campus at ninety-five
eras.blurb: Today's campus against the same mountains: parking structures, the pedestrian bridge over Verdugo Road, and the freeway in its cut behind the hill.
eras.credit: USDA NAIP orthoimagery, May 2022, public domain.
